entities.name: agni
entities.cite: fire—which is not what the grammatical rule designates

axioms.name: nibandhana
axioms.cite: the agni-word, founded on the agni-word, related to the agni-sruti

axioms.name: sambandha
axioms.cite: the rule's mention of it stands to it as name to named

axioms.name: atmadesananukulata
axioms.cite: language here bends back on itself, one occurrence functioning as designator and the form itself as designatum

denials.name: abhidheya
denials.cite: the item the rule mentions is not fire and not any particular utterance

claims.name: nibandhana
claims.cite: this word 'agni', founded upon the word 'agni' itself

claims.name: sambandha
claims.cite: enters into relation with the word-form 'agni' as what is expressed by it

rejected_readings.label: fire_as_designatum
rejected_readings.rendering: The word 'agni' achieves self-reference by ultimately designating fire itself.
rejected_readings.why: The commentary explicitly states: 'the item the rule mentions is not fire and not any particular utterance—it is the word-form itself.' This reading inverts the designatum, placing fire where the word-form should be.

rejected_readings.label: utterance_token_circularity
rejected_readings.rendering: A particular phonetic event 'agni' is self-grounded: this token refers to and depends upon itself.
rejected_readings.why: Confuses the linguistic word-form (a stable type recognized by grammar) with a particular utterance-token (an individual sound event). The verse's nibandhana applies to the word-form as a linguistic item, not to tokens. Treats a linguistic item as a mere phonetic property.

notes: Verse 1.60 exemplifies linguistic self-reference: the word 'agni', grounded in the word-form 'agni', systematically designates that very form rather than fire. This demonstrates that self-presentation belongs to language's nature and is not exceptional.
